name: MacTutor
born: 21 April 1869 Elze, Hanover, Prussia, now Germany
biography:
Philipp Furtw횄짚ngler 's parents were Wilhelm Furtw횄짚ngler (1829 - 1883) and Mathilde Sander ( born 1843) . Wilhelm was an organ builder as had been his father, the grandfather of the mathematician whose biography is being given, who was also named Philipp Furtw횄짚ngler (1800 - 1867) . The Furtw횄짚ngler family contains many famous people in addition to those just mentioned, for example two of the most prominent are the archaeologist Adolf Furtw횄짚ngler (1853 - 1907) and his son Wilhelm Furtw횄짚ngler (1886 - 1954) , a famous German conductor who was one of the leading exponents of Romantic music. Wilhelm and Mathilde had two sons, Philipp Furtw횄짚ngler the subject of this biography born 1869 and Wilhelm Furtw횄짚ngler (1875 - 1959) , who like his father and grandfather built organs.
Furtw횄짚ngler attended elementary school in his home town of Elze and then he studied at the Andreanum Gymnasium in Hildesheim. This Gymnasium was the nearest to Elze, with Hildesheim being only about 12 km distant. He graduated from the Gymnasium at Easter 1889 and, later that year, he began his study of mathematics at Georg-August University of G횄쨋ttingen. This was perhaps the best university in the world for mathematics at this time and he was taught by a number of leading mathematicians; Heinrich B횄쩌rkhardt , Robert Fricke , Otto H횄쨋lder , Felix Klein , Arthur Sch횄쨋nflies and Wilhelm Weber . In addition to mathematics, which formed his main interest, he also studied courses in physics and chemistry. Furtw횄짚ngler attended Felix Klein 's number theory course which went on for two semesters. This course particularly interested him and Klein suggested that he might undertake research on ternary cubic forms for his doctorate. Furtw횄짚ngler wrote the thesis Zur Theorie der in Linearfaktoren zerlegbaren ganzzahlingen tern횄짚ren kubischen Formen 창혫혟 ( On the theory of integers decomposable into linear factors of ternary cubic forms ) and he was awarded his doctorate by the University of G횄쨋ttingen for this work in 1895 . This thesis was published as a 64 -page monograph in 1896 . This was not his first publication for he had published Zur Begr횄쩌ndung der Idealtheorie 창혫혟 ( In support of ideal theory ) in 1895 . In fact, he had completed his studies at G횄쨋ttingen in 1894 and he spent the academic year 1894 - 95 as an assistant at the Physics Institute of the Technical University of Darmstadt.
At the University of G횄쨋ttingen, Furtw횄짚ngler had qualified to teach mathematics, physics, chemistry and mineralogy at secondary schools. However, before beginning secondary school teaching, he spent a year doing voluntary military service in a programme specially designed for those who had undertaken higher education. After this year, he spent two years teaching in schools in Hanover, Norden, a town on the North Sea shore, and Celle which is situated in north-central Germany. The city of Celle is on the Aller River, at the southern edge of the L횄쩌neburger Heide, northeast of Hanover. After this necessary teaching experience to complete his teaching qualifications, in 1898 he was appointed as an assistant at the Geodetic Institute in Potsdam. He was promoted to research associate and remained there until 1904 . In this post he carried out, in collaboration with Friedrich K횄쩌hnen, measurements of absolute masses and measurements of the gravitational constant at various locations in Silesia. He contributed the article Die Mechanik der einfachsten physikalischen Apparate in Versuchsanordnungen 창혫혟 ( The mechanics of the simplest physical apparatus in test arrangements ) to the Encyklop횄짚die der mathematischen Wissenschaften 창혫혟 ( Encyclopedia of mathematical sciences ) which was published in 1904 . While undertaking this work, he married Ella Buchwald in 1903 ; they had one daughter.
Despite working at the Geodetic Institute in Potsdam, it was number theory which was Furtw횄짚ngler's main research interest. His 80 -page paper 횄혵ber das Reziprozit횄짚tsgesetz der l-ten Potenzreste in alqebraischen Zahlk횄쨋rpern, wenn l eine ungerade Primzahl bedeutet 창혫혟 ( On the reciprocity of the l-th power residues in algebraic number fields, if l is an odd prime ) was awarded a prize by the G횄쨋ttingen Academy of Sciences . This was a contribution to Hilbert 's Ninth Problem, stated in his address at the International Congress of Mathematicians in Paris in 1900 . David Hilbert presented the following form of the Ninth Problem in his paper in the conference Proceedings:- Proof of the most general reciprocity law.
For a number field, it is to be proved the most general reciprocity law for the p th power residues, when p denotes an odd prime, and moreover, when p is a power of 2 or a power of an odd prime. The law, as well as the means essential to its proof, will, I believe, result from suitably generalizing the theory of the field of the p th roots of unity, developed by me, and any theory of relative quadratic fields. Furtw횄짚ngler continued to attack Hilbert 's Ninth Problem in a series of papers: Allgemeiner Existenzbeweis f횄쩌r den Klassenk횄쨋rper eines beliebigen algebraischen Zahlk횄쨋rpers 창혫혟 ( General existence proof for the class field of an arbitrary algebraic number field ) (1907) ; Reziprozit횄짚tsgesetze f횄쩌r Potenzreste mit Primzahlexponenten in algebraischen Zahlk횄쨋rpern I 창혫혟 ( Reciprocity laws for power residues with prime exponent in algebraic number I ) , (1909) ; Reziprozit횄짚tsgesetze f횄쩌r Potenzreste mit Primzahlexponenten in algebraischen Zahlk횄쨋rpern II 창혫혟 ( Reciprocity laws for power residues with prime exponent in algebraic number II ) (1912) ; and Reziprozit횄짚tsgesetze f횄쩌r Potenzreste mit Primzahlexponenten in algebraischen Zahlk횄쨋rpern III 창혫혟 ( Reciprocity laws for power residues with prime exponent in algebraic number III ) , (1913) .
Let us return to our description of Furtw횄짚ngler's career. He had qualified as a secondary school teacher and then worked at the Geodetic Institute in Potsdam but he had never habilitated . However, he was appointed as a professor at the Agricultural Academy in Bonn in 1904 and worked there for three years before moving to Aachen where he was a professor of mathematics at the Technische Hochschule from 1907 to 1910 . He then returned to the Agricultural Academy in Bonn as a professor of mathematics where he taught during 1910 - 1912 while at the same time held a lectureship in applied mathematics at the University of Bonn. In 1912 he was appointed as a professor of mathematics at the University of Vienna and he continued to hold this post until he retired in 1938 . In this role he worked very hard. Furtw횄짚ngler came to the Mathematical Institute at 8 clock in the morning and held oral examinations and colloquia. From 10 o'clock he held a one-hour seminar twice weekly. He regularly delivered lectures on differential and integral calculus, on number theory and on algebra. These lectures were very well attended; more than four hundred students attended his lectures but only half of them could get seats in the lecture theatre. This was all the more remarkable since, from 1916 he became progressively more paralysed and lectured from a wheelchair. He lectured without notes and had an assistant who wrote equations on the blackboard. It was Furtw횄짚ngler's lectures which turned Kurt G횄쨋del from physics to mathematics. G횄쨋del , who entered the University of Vienna in 1922 , described Furtw횄짚ngler's lectures as the best he had ever heard. To hear this quality of lectures from someone confined to a wheelchair who was paralysed from the neck down, would make a big impact on any student, but on G횄쨋del who was very conscious of his own health, it had an even bigger influence.
Furtw횄짚ngler was also a member of the examination committee for teaching in secondary schools. This position gave him a major amount of work since he had to hold a very large number of teacher training exams. It is remarkable that someone with his severe disability was able to carry out this high work load which would have seemed too great for a fully able person. He supervised the doctoral dissertations of 60 students including Otto Schreier and Olga Taussky-Todd . The obituary [ 4 ] lists 49 items by Furtw횄짚ngler. These include 횄혵ber das Verhalten der Ideale des Grundk횄쨋rpers im Klassenk횄쨋rper 창혫혟 ( On the behavior of the ideals of the base body in the class field ) (1916) , Punktgitter und Idealtheorie 창혫혟 ( Lattice of points and ideal theory ) (1921) , 횄혵ber die linearen Mannigfaltigkeiten auf Hyperfl횄짚chen zweiter Ordnung 창혫혟 ( On linear manifolds on hypersurfaces of the second order ) (1926) , 횄혵ber die simultane Approximation von Irrationalzahlen (1927) , 횄혵ber die simultane Approximation von Irrationalzahlen 창혫혟 ( On the simultaneous approximation of irrational numbers ) (1928) , and 횄혵ber affektfreie Gleichungen 창혫혟 ( About effectively free equations ) (1929) .
Perhaps, to my [ EFR ] mind, his greatest achievement, however, is his proof of the principal ideal theorem which answered a conjecture made by David Hilbert . This was a major unsolved problem which many leading algebraists had failed to solve. When Furtw횄짚ngler gave his proof he was 61 years old, remarkably old for someone to come up with such a major result. His 23 -page paper proving the principal ideal theorem is Beweis des Hauptidealsatzes f횄쩌r Klassenk횄쨋rper algebraischer Zahlk횄쨋rper 창혫혟 ( Proof of the principal ideal theorem of the class fields of algebraic number fields ) (1929) . For this outstanding work he was awarded the Ernst Albe memorial prize and the medal of the Carl Zeiss Foundation in 1930 . It is worth noting that this was only the second time that the Ernst Albe memorial prize had been awarded for work in pure mathematics, the previous occasion being in 1924 to Felix Klein .
Certainly these prizes were not the first honours to be given to Furtw횄짚ngler. He was elected a corresponding member of the Academy of Sciences in Vienna in 1916 and became a full member in 1927 . He was elected a corresponding member of the Berlin Academy of Sciences in 1931 , and a member of the German Academy of Scientists Leopoldina in Halle in 1939 . In the same year of 1939 , the 48 th volume of Monatshefte f횄쩌r Mathematik und Physik 창혫혟 ( Mathematics and physics monthly ) was dedicated to Furtw횄짚ngler on the occasion of his 70 th birthday.
Furtw횄짚ngler's second marriage was in 1929 to Emilie Sch횄쨋n. By this time he had been paralysed for over ten years but was still managing to carry out his duties despite being seriously ill. Emilie nursed him through more than ten years in a totally self-sacrificing way and helped him through his severe suffering which she made easier for him to bear. In October 1938 Furtw횄짚ngler's condition forced him to retire from his duties. In January 1940 his health became even worse and everyone realised that he did not have long to live. He survived until May of that year when he suffered a stroke.
Finally, let us mention that he was writing the article Allgemeine Theorie der algebraischen Zahlen 창혫혟 ( The general theory of algebraic numbers ) in 1938 , the year in which he was forced to retire. However, his health was too poor to allow him to complete the work which was intended for the Enzyklop횄짚die der mathematischen Wissenschaften 창혫혟 ( Encyclopedia of mathematical sciences ) . Furtw횄짚ngler's article was completed by Martin Eichler , Helmut Hasse and Wolfram Jehne. Kenkichi Iwasawa writes in a review of the article, which finally appeared in the 1953 edition of the Encyclopaedia :- It covers that part of algebraic number theory which is concerned with properties of finite algebraic number fields in general; class field theory, the theory of complex multiplications and other special theories on algebraic number fields are to be given in other articles. The material is well prepared and neatly arranged in a relatively small space. The main contents are as follows. 1 . The ideal theory of algebraic number fields; methods of Dedekind and Kronecker . 2 . Kummer - Hensel 's method with valuation theory. 3 . The structure of residue-class rings modulo integral ideals. 4 . The ramification theory. 5 . Ideal classes and units. 6 . Decomposable forms and Klein 's lattices. 7 . Orders and their conductors. 8 . Artin 's conductors and Artin 's L L L -functions. 9 . Calculation of class numbers by analytic method.
